comment_text.sn35.55:1.2: Throughout this section, only anicca and anattā are mentioned. Parallel passages with dukkha may have been lost to abbreviation. Each of the characteristics includes the others, but they are taught according to the inclination of the meditator.

root_text.sn35.55:1.1: “Kathaṁ nu kho, bhante, jānato, kathaṁ passato saṁyojanā samugghātaṁ gacchantī”ti?
root_text.sn35.55:1.2: “Cakkhuṁ kho, bhikkhu, anattato jānato passato saṁyojanā samugghātaṁ gacchanti.
root_text.sn35.55:1.6: yampidaṁ cakkhusamphassapaccayā uppajjati vedayitaṁ sukhaṁ vā dukkhaṁ vā adukkhamasukhaṁ vā tampi anattato jānato passato saṁyojanā samugghātaṁ gacchanti.
root_text.sn35.55:1.15: yampidaṁ manosamphassapaccayā uppajjati vedayitaṁ sukhaṁ vā dukkhaṁ vā adukkhamasukhaṁ vā tampi anattato jānato passato saṁyojanā samugghātaṁ gacchanti.
root_text.sn35.55:1.16: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhu, jānato evaṁ passato saṁyojanā samugghātaṁ gacchantī”ti.

translation_text.sn35.55:1.1: “Sir, how does one know and see so that the fetters are uprooted?”
translation_text.sn35.55:1.2: “Mendicant, knowing and seeing the eye as not-self, the fetters are uprooted …”
